topics.title: Solubility shifts with temperature: solids up, gases down; the solubility curve

voice_context.context: Solubility curve slide, taught in an encouraging, methodical voice. Core ideas, unchanged: a solubility curve plots the maximum mass of solute per 100 g of solvent (water) against temperature; the denominator is the solvent, unlike concentration where it was the whole solution. On the curve = saturated, below = unsaturated, above = supersaturated. Most solids rise with temperature; gases fall (warm Coke goes flat, fish need cool water). Steepness of the curve = temperature-sensitivity: sugar is steep, table salt is flat.

topics.title: Dissolving heats up or cools down — the why behind the solubility curve

voice_context.context: Dissolving as an energy tug-of-war: two pulls cost energy, the snap gives energy back; the balance decides warm vs cold, and the same logic explains why solid solubility rises and gas solubility falls with temperature.

topics.title: Like dissolves like: the three molecular tugs (solvent–solvent, solute–solute, solute–solvent)

voice_context.context: Slide on like-dissolves-like via three tugs: breaking solvent apart (cost), breaking solute apart (cost), forming new solute-solvent attractions (gives energy back). Dissolves when step 3 >= step 1 + step 2, which happens when substances are similar (both polar or both non-polar).

topics.title: Why the Crystals Come Out Pure

voice_context.context: This slide explains why crystals that fall out of a cooling solution come out pure, even when the original solution is dirty with impurity. The mechanism: only a substance that is SATURATED — packed right up against its solubility ceiling — gets forced out when the ceiling drops on cooling. Here the sugar holds 287 g and its ceiling falls from 287 g at 60 °C down to 180 g at 20 °C, so the excess (up to 107 g) has nowhere to stay and crystallises as pure square crystals. The impurity is only 5 g while its ceiling is about 210 g, so it is nowhere near saturated; cooling never forces it out and it stays dissolved in the liquid. The one idea to leave with: crystallisation SEPARATES — the solid that drops out is purer than the mixture you started with, because only the crowded, saturated component is pushed past its limit. First named misconception: 'cooling drags everything out of solution together' — students think a falling temperature precipitates all dissolved species; the slide corrects this by showing the impurity dots keep drifting, never settling, because 5 g sits far below the 210 g ceiling. Second misconception: 'the crystals must be dirty because they grew in a dirty liquid' — students assume the solid traps the colour; the readouts show 0 g of impurity is forced out, so the colour is left behind in the mother liquor and the crystals are clean white. The interactive shows a beaker of dirty syrup with a temperature slider from 20 °C to 60 °C. As you cool it, the sugar ceiling readout drops, the 'forced out' grams climb, and yellow sugar particles settle into a pile of square crystals, while pink impurity particles always drift dissolved. At 60 °C nothing is over the ceiling and a verdict tells you to cool it down; cooling flips the verdict to 'pure sugar crystals dropping out — the impurity stays dissolved.' Probing questions: If you cooled to 20 °C, how many grams of sugar are forced out and how many grams of impurity? What would happen if the impurity were also saturated at its ceiling? Why does the colour stay in the liquid rather than in the crystals? This builds on 'The Falling Ceiling: Cooling Forces a Solid Out,' which established how a dropping solubility ceiling forces excess solid out, and connects forward to 'Cool It or Boil It? Curve Shape Decides,' which asks when cooling versus evaporating is the better route to crystals.

topics.title: The Tyndall effect: colloids and suspensions scatter light, solutions do not

voice_context.context: Tyndall effect: shine a laser through a liquid and observe from the side. Solutions show no beam path (particles <1nm, too small to scatter); colloids (1-1000nm) and suspensions (>1000nm) light up the beam path. Looking clear does not mean a solution.

topics.title: THE FRAME: the X↔Y separation key (teach explicitly, before any method)

voice_context.context: Slide teaches the X-Y separation frame: every separation finds a physical property where parts differ, then exploits it. Classify first — homogeneous (uniform solution) unlocks molecular methods, heterogeneous (visible bits) unlocks bulk methods. A solution forbids bulk methods because no separate bits exist macroscopically. When several methods fit, pick the property with the biggest difference.

voice_context.context: Chromatography slide: separation by differing adsorption affinity; chain affinity→speed→position→separated; stationary vs mobile phase; misconception sticky=fast.

topics.title: Immiscibility / density differs → separating funnel + centrifugation

voice_context.context: Separating funnel used to separate immiscible liquids of differing density; denser layer drained from bottom via stopcock. Method fails for miscible liquids or equal-density immiscible liquids.

topics.title: Centrifugation: Density on Fast-Forward

voice_context.context: Centrifugation uses fast spinning to create super-gravity that separates fine suspended particles by density. Effective gravity grows with the square of rpm: g=(rpm/4000)squared x1800. Density is still the property doing the work; a centrifuge handles fine solids in liquid that won't settle, while a separating funnel needs liquids that already layer.

topics.title: One part sublimes → sublimation

voice_context.context: Slide on sublimation as a separation technique: solid skips liquid and goes straight to vapour, deposition is the reverse, and a mixture separates only when one component sublimes while the other stays solid.

topics.title: Tiny particles won't settle → coagulation (+ sedimentation, decantation, filtration)

voice_context.context: Slide on coagulation: tiny suspended particles slip through filters and never settle because they are smaller than filter pores and kept aloft by water molecule collisions and like-charge repulsion. A coagulant (alum/fitkari) cancels the charge so particles clump into large flocs that then sediment and can be decanted/filtered. Same principle makes paneer from milk using lemon/vinegar.

evaluation.explanation: Only salt-water and sugar syrup have uniform composition throughout — that's 2. Counting oil-on-water as homogeneous because it's clear would wrongly give 3.

options.text: Mars and India have the same temperature
options.label: D

questions.question: Water from a tap in India and ice from the polar caps of Mars have the exact same composition. This is because:

evaluation.explanation: The fixed-ratio recipe of a compound doesn't bend with location — that's the Law of Definite Proportions. A is wrong because water is a compound, not a mixture; B and D miss the underlying law.

options.text: All the hydrogen reacts because it is in excess
options.label: A

options.text: Only the 2:1 amount reacts; the extra hydrogen is left untouched
options.label: B

options.text: The extra hydrogen forces more oxygen into the water
options.label: C

options.text: The ratio shifts to use up all the hydrogen
options.label: D

questions.question: A student pours in a HUGE amount of hydrogen and just a little oxygen, expecting all the hydrogen to be used because there is so much of it. What actually happens?

evaluation.explanation: The reaction stops once the 2:1 recipe is satisfied — extra hydrogen just sits there unchanged. A, C and D all assume the fixed ratio can bend, which it cannot.

options.text: A mixture has a fixed recipe; a compound's ratio can vary
options.label: A

options.text: Both always have a fixed, unchangeable ratio
options.label: B

options.text: A mixture lets you choose the proportions; a compound has a fixed composition you can't change
options.label: C

options.text: Neither has any particular ratio
options.label: D

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes a mixture from a compound like water?

evaluation.explanation: A mixture is a variable ingredient list (a little or a lot of salt in water), while a compound follows a fixed recipe. A reverses the two; B and D ignore that mixtures vary while compounds don't.

options.text: The iron and sulfur chemically united into one new substance
options.label: A

options.text: It is a mixture — each substance kept its own properties and only shares space
options.label: B

options.text: It is a compound because two elements are present
options.label: C

options.text: It must be a pure element since the magnet works
options.label: D

questions.question: Iron filings are stirred into sulfur powder. A magnet still pulls the iron straight out. At the particle level, what does this tell you?

evaluation.explanation: The magnet still grabbing the iron proves each substance kept its identity and properties — that is a mixture. It is NOT a compound: 'two elements present' isn't enough; a compound forms only when they chemically unite into a brand-new substance (FeS), where the magnet would do nothing.

options.text: They formed a compound — a single new substance whose originals are gone
options.label: A

options.text: They stayed a mixture, just packed more tightly
options.label: B

options.text: The iron evaporated, leaving only sulfur
options.label: C

options.text: Nothing chemical — heating only changed the colour
options.label: D

questions.question: Iron and sulfur are heated together and lock into one repeating crystal lattice (FeS); the magnet no longer works. What happened?

evaluation.explanation: Chemically uniting into one new substance with new properties (magnet no longer works) means a compound formed. It is not a 'tighter mixture' — the iron's own magnetic property is gone because the original substances no longer exist.

options.text: Because it is a single new substance
options.label: A

options.text: Because zooming in reveals fat globules floating in watery fluid — not uniform at the molecular level
options.label: B

options.text: Because the eye-test already shows it is not uniform
options.label: C

options.text: Because all liquids are heterogeneous
options.label: D

questions.question: Milk looks like one smooth, even creamy liquid to the naked eye. Why is it actually classified as heterogeneous?

evaluation.explanation: Looking uniform to the eye is the trap; true homogeneity must hold at the molecular level. Zoom in and milk shows separate fat globules, so it is heterogeneous. The eye-test (C) is exactly what misleads here, and not all liquids are heterogeneous (D).

options.text: Whether it looks smooth and uniform to the naked eye
options.label: A

options.text: Whether it stays identical no matter how far you zoom or pan at the molecular level
options.label: B

options.text: Whether it is a liquid rather than a solid
options.label: C

options.text: Whether it contains more than one substance
options.label: D

questions.question: Salt water and milk both look like one even liquid. What truly decides whether each is homogeneous?

evaluation.explanation: Homogeneity means uniform at the molecular level — identical however far you zoom or pan. Salt water passes (ions sit evenly), milk fails (fat globules). The naked-eye look (A) is the very trap the slide warns against, and containing more than one substance (D) doesn't decide it — salt water is a mixture yet still homogeneous.

options.text: A homogeneous mixture
options.label: A

options.text: A compound
options.label: B

options.text: A heterogeneous mixture
options.label: C

options.text: A pure element
options.label: D

questions.question: Zooming into salt water shows Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions sitting evenly among the water particles, and it looks the same everywhere you pan. How is salt water best described?

evaluation.explanation: The ions keep their identity (you can boil the water off and get the salt back), so it is a mixture; and it is uniform everywhere at the molecular level, so it is homogeneous. It is not a compound because nothing chemically united into a new substance.

options.text: A homogeneous mixture of hydrogen and oxygen
options.label: A

options.text: A compound — one brand-new substance
options.label: B

options.text: A heterogeneous mixture
options.label: C

options.text: Two elements sharing space
options.label: D

questions.question: Zoom right into pure water and every single particle is the same H₂O molecule. What does this make it?

evaluation.explanation: Hydrogen and oxygen gave up their identities to form a single new substance, H₂O — that is a compound. It is not a mixture (A, D): the original elements no longer exist separately, they are chemically united.

options.text: A mixture is always a liquid; a compound is always a solid
options.label: A

options.text: In a mixture particles only share space and keep their properties; in a compound they chemically unite into a new substance
options.label: B

options.text: A mixture has one kind of particle; a compound has many
options.label: C

options.text: A compound can be separated by a magnet, a mixture cannot
options.label: D

questions.question: At the particle level, what is the key difference between a mixture and a compound?

evaluation.explanation: The defining test is identity: mixtures share space and keep their own properties (separable physically), while compounds chemically unite into one brand-new substance. State (A) is irrelevant; it is the mixture, not the compound, whose components a magnet can pull apart (D).

options.text: The blend looks uniform to the naked eye
options.label: A

options.text: The original substances' properties have disappeared and a new substance with new properties exists
options.label: B

options.text: You can still pick out the original substances with a magnet or filter
options.label: C

options.text: It is made of more than one element
options.label: D

questions.question: Which observation is the clearest sign that two substances formed a COMPOUND rather than a mixture?

evaluation.explanation: A compound means the originals chemically united, so their properties vanish and a new substance appears. Looking uniform (A) only fools the eye, being separable (C) is the sign of a mixture, and merely containing several elements (D) doesn't prove chemical union.

questions.question: You dissolve 8 g of sugar in 92 g of water. What is the concentration (w/w)?

evaluation.explanation: concentration = solute ÷ (solute + solvent) = 8 ÷ (8+92) = 8/100 = 8.0%. Option A (8/92) wrongly divides by the solvent instead of the whole solution.

questions.question: A solution contains 6 g of salt in 194 g of water. What is its concentration (w/w)?

evaluation.explanation: concentration = 6 ÷ (6+194) = 6/200 = 3.0%. Option B (6/194) divides by solvent only; the denominator must be the whole solution.

options.text: Water is the solute because it dissolves things
options.label: A

options.text: Sugar is the solute because it is the smaller amount that dissolves
options.label: B

options.text: Sugar is the solvent because it gives the taste
options.label: C

options.text: The solution is only the sugar, not the mixture
options.label: D

questions.question: In a cup of sugar water, which statement is correct?

evaluation.explanation: The solute is the substance present in the smaller amount (sugar); the solvent is the larger amount that does the dissolving (water). Option A swaps the roles.

options.text: Any liquid that contains water
options.label: A

options.text: A substance that has not yet dissolved
options.label: B

options.text: A homogeneous (even) mixture of two or more substances
options.label: C

options.text: Only a mixture that is liquid
options.label: D

questions.question: Which of these is the best definition of a solution?

evaluation.explanation: A solution is a homogeneous mixture and may be gaseous (air), solid (an alloy) or liquid. Option D is wrong because solutions need not be liquid.

options.text: The amount of solute increases
options.label: A

options.text: The solute amount stays the same but the concentration falls
options.label: B

options.text: Both the solute amount and the concentration rise
options.label: C

options.text: The concentration stays exactly the same
options.label: D

questions.question: You take a solution and add more water only (dilution). What happens?

evaluation.explanation: Dilution adds solvent only — the solute is untouched, so its share of the larger whole drops, lowering concentration. Option A is the trap: adding water does not add solute.

options.text: It becomes more concentrated and could enter the harm band
options.label: A

options.text: It becomes more dilute and could drop below the useful band
options.label: B

options.text: Nothing changes because no solute was added
options.label: C

options.text: The solute amount falls along with the concentration
options.label: D

questions.question: A solution sits in the 'works' band. You press 'Dilute (+30 g water)'. Which effect is possible?

evaluation.explanation: Adding solvent lowers concentration, so it can slip out of the useful band into 'too dilute'. Option C is wrong: concentration depends on the whole, which grew even though solute held steady.

options.text: Yes, salt is always harmful
options.label: A

options.text: No, salt water is always safe to drink
options.label: B

options.text: It depends on the concentration — at what amount?
options.label: C

options.text: It depends only on which brand of salt is used
options.label: D

questions.question: Is salt water dangerous? What is the scientist's best response?

evaluation.explanation: Safety, usefulness and harm belong to an amount, not an ingredient. The right question is 'at what concentration?' Options A and B fix danger to the ingredient itself, which the slide warns against.

options.text: The special ingredients that only ORS contains
options.label: A

options.text: The right proportion of those everyday ingredients
options.label: B

options.text: Having as much salt as possible
options.label: C

options.text: Using only water and nothing else
options.label: D

questions.question: ORS rehydrates a child using just sugar, salt and water. What actually makes it work?

evaluation.explanation: Same three ingredients every time — only the correct proportion rehydrates. Option C is the misconception: too much salt harms rather than helps; amounts decide the effect, not the ingredient list.

options.text: 5% m/v
options.label: A

options.text: 5% v/v
options.label: B

options.text: 5% m/m
options.label: C

questions.question: You dissolve 5 g of silver nitrate (solid) and top up to a total of 100 mL of solution. What is the concentration?

evaluation.explanation: Solid weighed in grams over volume of solution in mL gives m/v: (5 g / 100 mL) x 100 = 5% m/v. It is not v/v (the solute is weighed, not poured) nor m/m (the whole is measured by volume).

options.text: 5% v/v
options.label: A

options.text: 5% m/v
options.label: B

options.text: 5.3% v/v (5 mL solute / 95 mL solvent)
options.label: C

options.text: 5% m/m
options.label: D

questions.question: You mix 5 mL of methanol with solvent to make 100 mL of solution. What is the concentration?

evaluation.explanation: Both are liquids in mL, so v/v: (5 mL / 100 mL) x 100 = 5% v/v. Option C wrongly divides by the solvent (95 mL); the denominator must be the whole solution, not just the solvent.

options.text: solvent only
options.label: A

options.text: solute only
options.label: B

options.text: the whole solution (solute + solvent)
options.label: C

options.text: water added
options.label: D

questions.question: In every one of these percent expressions, the denominator is the amount of:

evaluation.explanation: The denominator is always the whole solution, solute included. Using solvent only is the classic error — adding solute raises both the top and the bottom of the fraction.

options.text: It stays the same because only solvent counts
options.label: A

options.text: It increases, because the solution total includes the solute
options.label: B

options.text: It decreases
options.label: C

options.text: It becomes zero
options.label: D

questions.question: You add more solute to a beaker. What happens to the denominator of the percent expression?

evaluation.explanation: The whole solution is solute + solvent, so adding solute lifts the denominator too. Thinking only solvent counts (A) is the misconception the slide warns against.

options.text: How you measured each amount on the bench (weighed vs poured)
options.label: A

options.text: A fixed property of the substance itself
options.label: B

options.text: The temperature of the room
options.label: C

options.text: Whichever gives the biggest percentage
options.label: D

questions.question: What decides whether you report a concentration as m/m, m/v or v/v?

evaluation.explanation: The metric is a choice driven by how you measured — solids weighed in grams, liquids poured in mL. It is not a fixed property of the substance (B); the same solution can carry different tags.

options.text: Because in m/v grams and millilitres do not cancel
options.label: A

options.text: Because m/v is always temperature dependent
options.label: B

options.text: Because m/v uses a different formula
options.label: C

options.text: Because m/v measures solvent, not solution
options.label: D

questions.question: Why must units be stated for % m/v, but not for % v/v or % m/m?

evaluation.explanation: In v/v and m/m the numerator and denominator share a unit so it cancels; in m/v grams over millilitres do not cancel, so g and mL must be stated. It uses the same (part/whole)x100 formula.

options.text: One of them made an arithmetic mistake
options.label: A

options.text: They are different solutions
options.label: B

options.text: Both are correct — only the denominator (mass vs volume) differs
options.label: C

options.text: The solution changed when poured
options.label: D

questions.question: The same NaOH solution is labelled 50% m/m by one chemist and 76.3% m/v by another. What is going on?

evaluation.explanation: Nothing in the bottle changed; the same solution can be 50% m/m and 76.3% m/v at once because the denominator differs (1000 g vs 1000 mL). That is exactly why the tag is never optional.

options.text: 5% is too dilute to be useful
options.label: A

options.text: Without the tag you cannot tell if it means g/g, g/mL or mL/mL
options.label: B

options.text: 5% only ever means m/m
options.label: C

options.text: Percentages are not allowed without temperature
options.label: D

questions.question: A bottle simply says "5%" with no tag. Why is this a problem?

evaluation.explanation: The number alone is ambiguous — 5% could be m/m, m/v or v/v, each meaning a different amount. Stripping the tag misleads; the slide stresses always writing m/m, m/v or v/v. It does not default to m/m (C).

options.text: 1 part acid : 5 parts water
options.label: A

options.text: 1 part acid : 6 parts water
options.label: B

options.text: 1 part acid : 2 parts water
options.label: C

options.text: 6 parts acid : 1 part water
options.label: D

questions.question: You want 2 M acid from a 12 M stock. What mixing ratio do you use?

evaluation.explanation: Dilution factor = 12 ÷ 2 = 6, so the mix is 6 parts total; 1 part is acid, leaving 6 − 1 = 5 parts water. Option B uses the factor 6 directly as water and forgets to subtract the 1 acid part.

options.text: 4 parts water
options.label: A

options.text: 3 parts water
options.label: B

options.text: 12 parts water
options.label: C

options.text: 9 parts water
options.label: D

questions.question: To make 3 M acid from a 12 M stock, how many parts of water per part of acid?

evaluation.explanation: Dilution factor = 12 ÷ 3 = 4 (total parts), so water = 4 − 1 = 3 parts. Option A mistakes the dilution factor itself for the water amount instead of subtracting the acid part.

options.text: Pure gold is too soft, so the ring bends, dents and scratches
options.label: A

options.text: Pure gold is too cheap to be worth wearing
options.label: B

options.text: Pure gold tarnishes and loses its shine quickly
options.label: C

options.text: Pure gold is poisonous to wear on skin
options.label: D

questions.question: Why is 24K gold a poor choice for an everyday ring even though it is the purest?

evaluation.explanation: Higher purity raises shine/value but lowers durability — at 24K the metal is too soft for daily wear, which is why 22K (alloyed) sits in the usable window. B is wrong: pure gold is the most valuable, not cheap.

options.text: There is a range that works, because raising composition helps one property while hurting another
options.label: A

options.text: The highest composition is always the best choice
options.label: B

options.text: Composition has no effect on a material's properties
options.label: C

options.text: The lowest composition is always the safest choice
options.label: D

questions.question: The slide says composition has 'a usable window, not a ranking.' What does this mean?

evaluation.explanation: Two properties pull in opposite directions as composition rises, so only a band of values is usable — no single value is 'best'. B and D are the ranking mindset the slide explicitly rejects.

options.text: More concentrated often just means more dangerous or wrong for the job
options.label: A

options.text: Correct — stronger acid is always the best to use
options.label: B

options.text: More concentrated acid always reacts more safely
options.label: C

options.text: Concentration does not affect how an acid behaves
options.label: D

questions.question: A student says 'more concentrated always means better acid.' What is the correct correction?

evaluation.explanation: The pitfall box warns that 'more concentrated' is not 'better' — 12 M stockroom acid is corrosive and must be diluted to the safe ~2 M window. B repeats the very misconception being corrected.

options.text: At 12 M it is too corrosive to handle safely, even though it reacts strongly
options.label: A

options.text: At 12 M it is too weak to react at all
options.label: B

options.text: Diluting it makes it react faster
options.label: C

options.text: 2 M acid is more concentrated than 12 M acid
options.label: D

questions.question: Why must stockroom acid be diluted to about 2 M before classroom use?

evaluation.explanation: As concentration rises, reaction strength goes up but 'safe to handle' drops — 12 M is strong yet dangerous, so you dilute into the usable band. B is backwards: 12 M is highly concentrated, not weak.

options.text: Is it safe to consume? Is it right for the job?
options.label: A

options.text: Is it expensive? Is it rare?
options.label: B

options.text: Is it a solid? Is it a liquid?
options.label: C

options.text: Who made it? When was it made?
options.label: D

questions.question: The slide says to ask two questions about any concentration. Which pair is correct?

evaluation.explanation: The slide pairs a 'Consume — is it safe?' question (e.g. 0.9% saline, AQI) with a 'Perform — is it right for the job?' question (e.g. carbon in steel, E10 fuel). The others are unrelated to composition decisions.

options.text: Perform — is this composition right for the job?
options.label: A

options.text: Consume — is this safe to swallow?
options.label: B

options.text: Ranking — which metal is best overall?
options.label: C

options.text: Cost — which is cheapest to make?
options.label: D

questions.question: 'Under 1% carbon turns soft iron into knife-hard steel.' This is an example of which question?

evaluation.explanation: Carbon content tuning iron into hard steel is about fitness for purpose (Perform), not safety to consume. B confuses it with the safety question, which applies to things like saline or air quality.

questions.question: In the simulation, 100 mL of cooking oil (density 0.91 g/mL) is poured. What mass does the scale read?

evaluation.explanation: mass = volume × density = 100 × 0.91 = 91 g. Option B is the volume-based guess (assuming 1 g per mL like water); oil packs less mass per mL.

questions.question: 50 mL of mercury has a density of 13.6 g/mL. What is its mass?

evaluation.explanation: mass = 50 × 13.6 = 680 g. Option D divides instead of multiplies (50/13.6), and 50 g ignores density entirely.

options.text: How heavy an object feels in your hand
options.label: A

options.text: How much mass is packed into a given volume
options.label: B

options.text: The total weight of a substance
options.label: C

options.text: The total volume a substance takes up
options.label: D

questions.question: What does density actually measure?

evaluation.explanation: Density = mass ÷ volume — mass packed per unit space. A and C confuse it with weight, and D confuses it with volume; both depend on amount, while density does not.

options.text: Length and time
options.label: A

options.text: Temperature and pressure
options.label: B

options.text: Mass (grams) and volume (millilitres)
options.label: C

options.text: Force and energy
options.label: D

questions.question: The slide calls density a 'bridge' between two measurements. Which two?

evaluation.explanation: Density translates between grams (the scale) and millilitres (the cylinder). The other pairs are unrelated quantities not connected by density.

options.text: They must have the same mass since the volumes are equal
options.label: A

options.text: The water is heavier because it is more common
options.label: B

options.text: The honey is heavier because it has greater density
options.label: C

options.text: You cannot say anything without weighing both
options.label: D

questions.question: 100 mL of honey and 100 mL of water reach the same line in the cylinder. What can you conclude about their masses?

evaluation.explanation: Equal volume does NOT mean equal mass — honey's higher density packs more mass into the same space. A is the exact misconception the slide warns against; equal volume only matches the line, not the weight.

options.text: It is correct — volume determines weight
options.label: A

options.text: Different liquids have different densities, so mass differs
options.label: B

options.text: The scale is unreliable for liquids
options.label: C

options.text: Volume cannot be measured accurately
options.label: D

questions.question: A student says 'same volume of any liquid will weigh the same on the scale.' Why is this wrong?

evaluation.explanation: Mass = volume × density, so even at equal volume, different densities give different masses (water 100 g, oil 91 g, mercury 1360 g for 100 mL). A repeats the misconception; the scale and cylinder are both fine.

options.text: The barrel has a higher density because it has more mercury
options.label: A

options.text: Both have the same density, 13.6 g/mL
options.label: B

options.text: The drop has a higher density because it is concentrated
options.label: C

options.text: Density cannot be defined for a single drop
options.label: D

questions.question: A single drop of mercury and a whole barrel of mercury are compared. Which is true?

evaluation.explanation: Density is a fixed signature of a substance — it never changes with amount. A and C wrongly assume more (or less) material changes density; mass and volume scale together, keeping the ratio constant.

options.text: Because density changes predictably with volume
options.label: A

options.text: Because density is a fixed value for a substance, so mass = volume × density
options.label: B

options.text: Because all liquids have a density of 1 g/mL
options.label: C

options.text: Because mass and volume are always equal
options.label: D

questions.question: Why can density be used as a conversion factor to find mass without a scale?

evaluation.explanation: Since density stays constant for a given substance, measuring volume and multiplying by density gives mass. A is false (density is fixed, not volume-dependent), and C only holds for water, not all liquids.

options.text: 0 g — it all dissolves
options.label: A

questions.question: At a certain temperature the solubility ceiling is 50 g per 100 g of water. If you stir 70 g of salt into 100 g of water, how much settles undissolved on the bottom?

evaluation.explanation: Only the ceiling (50 g) dissolves; the rest, 70 − 50 = 20 g, settles out. Choosing 0 g ignores that there is a fixed limit; 50 g is the amount dissolved, not the excess.

options.text: Unsaturated, with room for more
options.label: A

options.text: Exactly saturated — 30 g dissolved, 0 g undissolved
options.label: B

options.text: Saturated with 10 g undissolved
options.label: C

options.text: Supersaturated
options.label: D

questions.question: The ceiling is 30 g per 100 g of water. You add three 10-g spoons (30 g total). What is the state of the solution?

evaluation.explanation: Added (30 g) equals the ceiling (30 g), so all 30 g dissolves and the solution sits exactly at saturation with nothing left over. It is not unsaturated (no room left) and nothing is undissolved.

options.text: The maximum mass of solute that dissolves in a given quantity of solvent at a specific temperature
options.label: A

options.text: The amount of solute the experimenter chooses to add
options.label: B

options.text: Any mass of solute that disappears into water
options.label: C

options.text: The speed at which a solute dissolves
options.label: D

questions.question: Which statement correctly defines solubility?

evaluation.explanation: Solubility is a maximum tied to a fixed amount of solvent at a stated temperature. It is not set by the experimenter (B), is a ceiling not just 'any amount' (C), and describes a limit, not a rate (D).

options.text: Because temperature changes the maximum amount the solvent can hold
options.label: A

options.text: Because warm water weighs less than cold water
options.label: B

options.text: Because thermometers are needed to measure mass
options.label: C

options.text: Because the solute changes identity when heated
options.label: D

questions.question: Why does a solubility figure mean nothing unless a temperature is stated alongside it?

evaluation.explanation: The ceiling rises with temperature, so the number depends on it. Water's mass doesn't meaningfully change the limit (B), temperature isn't about measuring mass (C), and the solute keeps its identity (D).

options.text: Saturated but dilute
options.label: A

options.text: Unsaturated and dilute
options.label: B

options.text: Saturated and concentrated
options.label: C

options.text: Supersaturated
options.label: D

questions.question: A barely-soluble salt forms a solution that holds only a tiny amount of dissolved solute, yet no more will dissolve. This solution is best described as:

evaluation.explanation: 'Saturated' means no more can dissolve; 'dilute' means little is actually in solution — these measure different things, so both can be true at once. Saturated does NOT imply concentrated, which is the tempting trap in C.

options.text: For a poorly soluble salt the limit is very low, so it can be saturated yet still dilute
options.label: A

options.text: Saturated solutions are always dilute, never concentrated
options.label: B

options.text: Concentration and saturation are the same thing
options.label: C

options.text: A saturated solution has no solute dissolved in it
options.label: D

questions.question: A student says, 'A saturated solution must always be very concentrated.' Why is this wrong?

evaluation.explanation: Saturation describes 'no more can dissolve', while concentration describes 'how much is dissolved'. A low ceiling means saturation is reached while still dilute. B over-corrects, C confuses the two terms, and D is false — a saturated solution holds its maximum.

options.text: It temporarily holds more solute than the temperature normally allows, and is unstable
options.label: A

options.text: It holds exactly the maximum and is perfectly stable
options.label: B

options.text: It holds less solute than a saturated solution
options.label: C

options.text: It can never release solute back as solid
options.label: D

questions.question: What makes a supersaturated solution different from a saturated one?

evaluation.explanation: A supersaturated solution is coaxed past the normal ceiling, so it is unstable and the excess must eventually drop out as solid. B describes a saturated solution, C is backwards, and D contradicts its key behaviour.

options.text: It belongs to the solute–solvent–temperature pairing, not to the person stirring
options.label: A

options.text: It belongs to whoever decides how many spoons to add
options.label: B

options.text: It is fixed forever for a given salt regardless of conditions
options.label: C

options.text: It depends only on how fast you stir
options.label: D

questions.question: In the simulation, warming the water lets the same beaker dissolve more salt. What does this show about the solubility number?

evaluation.explanation: Raising temperature raised the ceiling, proving the number is set by the solute, solvent, and temperature together — not by the experimenter (B) and not fixed regardless of conditions (C). Stirring speeds dissolving but doesn't change the maximum (D).

options.text: How fast a solute dissolves at each temperature
options.label: A

options.text: The maximum mass of solute that dissolves in a fixed amount of solvent at each temperature
options.label: B

options.text: The boiling point of the solution at each concentration

questions.question: Whether particles settle out is a tug-of-war between two effects. Which two?

evaluation.explanation: Settling is decided by gravity dragging particles down against the restless molecules of the liquid that keep knocking them upward. The size sets which side wins. The other pairs play no role in this slide's model.

options.text: The settling one has larger, heavier particles so gravity overcomes molecular jostling
options.label: A

options.text: The settling one is at a higher temperature
options.label: B

options.text: The non-settling one contains no particles at all
options.label: C

options.text: The non-settling one is in a wider beaker
options.label: D

questions.question: In two mixtures with the same liquid, why does one settle and the other does not?

evaluation.explanation: Particle size is the single deciding variable: above ~1000 nm gravity wins and particles settle; below it, molecular jostling keeps them afloat. The non-settling mixture still has particles (B, C, D are irrelevant to the cause).

options.text: They are weightless, so gravity cannot act on them

options.text: They are small enough that Brownian motion re-suspends them faster than gravity pulls them down
options.label: B

options.text: There are no real particles, only liquid
options.label: D

questions.question: Milk stays uniformly white for days without forming a layer. What does this tell us about its particles?

evaluation.explanation: This is kinetic stability — the particles do have weight, but at colloid size molecular bombardment keeps re-suspending them. They are NOT weightless (A) and they are not dissolved into individual molecules like a true solution (C).

options.text: They do have mass; constant molecular jostling simply keeps them suspended (kinetic stability)
options.label: A

options.text: Colloid particles actually do settle within minutes
options.label: B

options.text: Colloids contain no particles, only dissolved molecules
options.label: C

options.text: Gravity does not exist on such small scales
options.label: D

questions.question: A student says 'colloid particles never settle, so they must have zero mass.' Why is this wrong?

evaluation.explanation: The particles have real mass — they stay up because Brownian motion re-suspends them faster than gravity can settle them, which is kinetic stability, not zero mass. Colloids do not settle (B), do contain genuine particles (C), and gravity still applies (D).

questions.question: Which mixture would leave particles trapped on filter paper AND have particles visible to the naked eye?

evaluation.explanation: Only a suspension (>1000 nm) has particles large enough to be visible and to be trapped by filter paper. Solution and colloid particles are invisible and pass straight through the filter.

questions.question: Both salt water and milk pass through filter paper and look free of visible grains. What single property explains both facts?

evaluation.explanation: Particle size under ~1000 nm makes particles invisible and small enough to slip through filter pores. Colour (B) is irrelevant, neither settles (C), and heating (D) plays no part.

questions.question: You shine a laser through a clear liquid and, looking from the side, you can clearly trace a glowing beam path through it. What can you conclude?

evaluation.explanation: A visible beam path means particles are large enough (>1 nm) to scatter light sideways — that is a colloid or suspension. A solution shows no beam path because its particles are too small to scatter.

options.text: The dissolved particles are too small (<1 nm) to scatter light

questions.question: A laser is fired through salt water (Liquid A). Viewed from the side, the path stays completely dark. Why?

evaluation.explanation: In a true solution the particles are smaller than 1 nm, too small to deflect light toward your eye, so no path is seen. It is not absorption — the beam passes straight through and exits the far side.

questions.question: A mixture's particles are measured at about 200 nm. Based on the size ranges, what type of mixture is it?

evaluation.explanation: Colloid particles fall in the 1–1000 nm range, so 200 nm is a colloid. It is above 1 nm so not a solution, and below 1000 nm so not a suspension.

questions.question: Chalk-in-water particles are larger than 1000 nm and scatter light strongly into a broad, cloudy band. This places the mixture in which category?

evaluation.explanation: Particles bigger than 1000 nm define a suspension, which scatters light strongly. A colloid is only 1–1000 nm, and a solution shows no scattering at all.

questions.question: Two glasses both look equally clear and colourless. A student says 'they must both be solutions because they look clear.' Why is this reasoning wrong?

evaluation.explanation: Appearance is misleading: a dilute colloid can look just as clear as a solution. You must trust the Tyndall test, not your first glance. 'Looks clear' does not mean 'solution'.

questions.question: Dilute starch water and salt water look identical to the eye. What is the BEST way to tell which one is the colloid?

evaluation.explanation: Since both look clear, appearance fails. The Tyndall test works: the colloid scatters light and shows a glowing beam path, while the salt-water solution stays dark.

questions.question: Seeing visible 'rays' of light from a cinema projector beam crossing a dusty room is an example of what?

evaluation.explanation: The visible beam is light scattering sideways off suspended dust particles — the same Tyndall physics, no laser needed. It is scattering, not absorption or any chemical change.

questions.question: A student asks 'if molecules don't scatter light, why is the sky blue?' What is the correct, precise response?

evaluation.explanation: Blue sky comes from Rayleigh scattering by air molecules — a related but distinct mechanism, not the Tyndall effect itself. Keeping the terms precise matters; the sky is not a colloid and the molecules scatter (not absorb) blue light.

questions.question: Milk is described as 'fat in water'. In this colloid, what is the dispersion medium?

evaluation.explanation: In 'phase in medium', the second word is the medium (carrier). Water carries the dispersed fat droplets, so water is the dispersion medium. Fat is the dispersed phase, not the medium.

options.text: Air — it surrounds everything

questions.question: Fog is 'water in air'. Which is the dispersed phase?

evaluation.explanation: The dispersed phase is the substance spread through the medium — here the water droplets. Air is the dispersion medium. Fog is colloidal, not a true solution.

questions.question: Colloidal particles fall in the size range 1–1000 nm. Which particle size below would behave as a colloid?

evaluation.explanation: 250 nm lies inside the 1–1000 nm colloidal range. 0.3 nm is solution-sized (truly dissolved); 5000 nm and 50000 nm are far too large and would settle out like a suspension.

questions.question: A mixture has particles about 800 nm across that stay evenly spread and never settle. How is it best classified?

evaluation.explanation: 800 nm sits inside the colloidal band of 1–1000 nm, and staying dispersed without settling is the hallmark of a colloid. True-solution particles are under ~1 nm; suspension particles are larger and settle out.

options.text: It makes oil dissolve completely into water, forming a true solution

questions.question: You shake oil and water with no soap, and they re-separate into layers. Adding soap then keeps them mixed. What does the soap (emulsifying agent) actually do?

evaluation.explanation: An emulsifier wraps each droplet, preventing droplets from merging back into a layer — so the emulsion holds. It does not dissolve the oil (it stays a dispersed phase, not a solution) and no chemical reaction occurs.

options.text: Without an emulsifier nothing stops droplets merging, so they coalesce and rise back into a layer
options.label: A

options.text: Soap permanently dissolves oil into water at the molecular level
options.label: B

options.text: Shaking adds soap automatically, so both end up stable
options.label: C

options.text: Oil and water are miscible, so they only separate by accident
options.label: D

questions.question: Why does a soap-free oil-and-water mixture re-separate after shaking, while a soapy one stays milky?

evaluation.explanation: Oil and water are immiscible; without an emulsifier the droplets re-merge and rise into layers. Soap stabilises by coating droplets — it does not truly dissolve the oil, and oil/water are not miscible.

options.text: So the cells settle to the bottom whenever you sit still
options.label: A

options.text: So the cells stay evenly dispersed and keep circulating instead of settling out
options.label: B

options.label: C

questions.question: Why is it important that blood is colloidal rather than a true suspension?

evaluation.explanation: Being colloidal keeps the cells evenly dispersed so circulation works. In a true suspension the cells would settle out at rest — exactly what you do NOT want. The cells are dispersed, not dissolved.

options.text: It heats the blood until the cells evaporate
options.label: A

options.text: It spins the sample hard enough to overcome the colloid's stability and force the cells down
options.label: B

options.label: C

options.text: It waits for the cells to settle naturally within seconds
options.label: D

questions.question: Blood cells normally stay dispersed and won't settle on their own. How does a centrifuge separate the cells from the plasma?

evaluation.explanation: A centrifuge spins fast enough to overcome the colloidal stability, forcing the dense cells to the bottom and leaving clear plasma above. Because the system is colloidal, the cells would not settle naturally on their own.

options.text: Particles carry a built-in pull toward disorder, so spreading out is the downhill, free direction
options.label: A

options.label: B

questions.question: A sugar cube spreads out on its own in hot tea but never reassembles into a cube. Why does mixing happen by itself while unmixing does not?

evaluation.explanation: Mixing is spontaneous because the natural tendency is toward disorder — it's the free downhill. B is wrong: nothing chemical happens when sugar dissolves, so it could in principle reform; the barrier is the energy cost, not a chemical change.

options.text: Mixing increases disorder and is spontaneous, while separating would decrease disorder and needs energy fed in
options.label: A

options.text: The gases react together to form a new compound
options.label: B

options.text: Gravity pulls the heavier gas down, locking them in place
options.label: C

options.text: Room temperature is too low for the gases to separate
options.label: D

questions.question: Two gases released in a room mix completely and never separate back to opposite ends. The best explanation is that:

evaluation.explanation: Spreading out raises disorder so it happens freely; reversing it fights the natural pull and demands energy. B is wrong — physical mixing is not a chemical reaction, so no new compound forms.

options.text: The particles drift back to mixed, because the pull toward disorder never switches off
options.label: A

options.text: They stay perfectly separated forever, since the work is already done
options.label: B

options.text: They become a new chemical that cannot remix
options.label: C

options.text: They separate even further on their own
options.label: D

questions.question: You feed energy in and pull the mixture fully apart into the PURE trays. According to the slide, what happens if you then stop supplying energy?

evaluation.explanation: Separation isn't a one-time push — you're holding particles apart against a pull that never stops, so they remix once you ease off. B is the tempting trap of thinking one push 'parks' them apart permanently.

options.text: Because the natural pull toward mixing is constant, so energy must keep being supplied to hold the particles apart
options.label: A

options.text: Because the first burst was simply too weak to finish the job
options.label: B

options.text: Because separation permanently changes the particles into a different substance
options.label: C

options.text: Because mixing also requires energy, which cancels the burst out
options.label: D

questions.question: Why is a single quick burst of energy NOT enough to keep a mixture separated?

evaluation.explanation: The pull to disorder is always on, so staying separated needs ongoing energy. B mistakes it for an issue of strength, and D is wrong because mixing is free — it costs no energy.

options.text: They are physical processes that use energy to overcome the tendency to stay mixed, not chemical reactions
options.label: A

options.text: They chemically break the substances down into elements
options.label: B

options.text: They work without any energy at all
options.label: C

options.text: They permanently change the identity of the substances being separated
options.label: D

questions.question: The slide says 'nothing chemical happened when they mixed, so nothing chemical is needed to un-mix.' What does this tell us about separation methods like distillation or filtration?

evaluation.explanation: Mixing was purely physical, so separation is too — an energy-driven physical process. B and D wrongly assume a chemical change; C is wrong because every separation method must be powered by energy.

options.text: Dissolving was a physical change, so the salt and water can be separated by feeding in energy (e.g. heat to evaporate), with no chemical needed
options.label: A

options.text: You actually cannot separate salt from water by any method
options.label: B

options.text: Salt water is a new compound that no longer contains salt or water
options.label: C

options.text: Separating it releases energy rather than requiring it
options.label: D

questions.question: A student claims you must add a reactant to chemically 'undo' a salt-water mixture. Why is this wrong?

evaluation.explanation: No chemical change occurred on mixing, so none is needed to reverse it — just energy like heat. C is wrong (the components keep their identities) and D is wrong because separation costs energy, it doesn't release it.

options.text: 80 units — each separation costs energy, but remixing in between was free

questions.question: On the simulator, dragging from 0 to 40 spends 40 units of energy. You then let them remix (back to 0) and drag from 0 to 40 again. What is the total energy spent unmixing?

evaluation.explanation: Each uphill separation costs 40 units, so two cost 80; the remix in between is free (downhill) but gives nothing back. B wrongly assumes separation is a one-time cost, and D wrongly charges energy for the free remixing step.

options.text: Remixing releases 0 J (it's free and spontaneous); the second separation costs another 50 J
options.label: A

options.text: Remixing releases 50 J, which pays for the next separation, so the second separation is free

options.label: D

questions.question: Separating one batch of a mixture costs 50 J of energy. After it spontaneously remixes, how much energy does that remixing release, and what does separating it a second time cost?

evaluation.explanation: Mixing is the free downhill — it costs nothing and gives no usable energy back, so each separation must be paid for again in full (50 J). B is the tempting trap of imagining the remix 'recharges' you to separate for free.

questions.question: According to the slide, what is the single logical move behind EVERY separation method?

evaluation.explanation: The slide's core rule: find a physical property where the parts differ, then exploit it. A is wrong because nothing chemical happened on mixing, so nothing chemical is needed to un-mix; heating (C) is just one specific exploit, and classifying (D) is a step, not the move itself.

questions.question: Why does the slide say 'no magic is involved' and no chemical reaction is needed to separate a mixture?

evaluation.explanation: The components keep their identities in a mixture — mixing was a physical event, so a physical property difference reverses it. A is false (mixtures aren't pure), and B/D invent requirements the slide never makes.

questions.question: You have a uniform (homogeneous) solution like salt water. Which toolkit of methods do you actually own?

evaluation.explanation: Homogeneous → molecular methods. A is the misconception that 'lots of liquid' means bulk; in a solution no separate bits exist to grab, so bulk methods have nothing to work with. Homogeneous mixtures CAN be separated (D wrong) — just with molecular methods.

questions.question: A mixture of sand and iron filings shows clearly visible separate bits. How is it classified and which toolkit unlocks?

evaluation.explanation: Visible separate bits = heterogeneous, which unlocks bulk methods (e.g. a magnet for the iron). The macroscopic clue of visible bits rules out a uniform solution, so molecular methods are not the family you reach for here.

options.text: Salt particles are too heavy to filter

options.label: D

questions.question: Why can you NOT use filtration to separate salt from salt water?

evaluation.explanation: A solution is homogeneous right down to the molecular level — macroscopically there are no separate bits, so a filter has nothing to trap. A wrongly imagines visible salt particles, and B/D are invented reasons; the real cause is the uniform microscopic structure that forbids bulk methods.

options.text: Ink and water are immiscible, so they form layers instead
options.label: A

questions.question: A student says 'I'll just let the ink settle out of water by leaving it still (sedimentation).' Why does this fail?

evaluation.explanation: Ink in water is homogeneous, so no separate particles exist to settle out — bulk methods like sedimentation have nothing to act on. A is wrong because they DO mix (not immiscible), and C/D misstate how settling works; the key idea is the uniform molecular structure of a solution.

questions.question: Two liquid mixtures need distillation. Mixture X has components boiling at 78 °C and 80 °C; mixture Y at 78 °C and 120 °C. Which separates more cleanly by distillation, and why?

evaluation.explanation: Pick the property where the parts differ MOST: Y's 42 °C gap means the lower-boiling part escapes well before the other, a far cleaner cut than X's 2 °C gap. A and D confuse 'lower' or 'similar' with 'easier' — it's the size of the difference, not the absolute values, that makes the cut sharp.

options.text: Choose whichever method is cheapest
options.label: A

options.text: Always pick distillation
options.label: B

options.text: Choose the property where the parts differ the most — the bigger the gap, the cleaner the cut
options.label: C

options.text: Use every method one after another
options.label: D

questions.question: When more than one separation method fits a mixture, what is the smart strategy the slide recommends?

evaluation.explanation: The slide says think like a scientist and exploit the property with the largest difference, because a bigger gap gives a cleaner separation. A and B fix on cost or one default method rather than the size of the difference, and D wastefully ignores the point of choosing the sharpest single tool.

questions.question: A solution holds 287 g of solid dissolved at 60°C. It is cooled to 40°C, where the water can only hold 241 g. How much solid crystallises out?

evaluation.explanation: excess out = dissolved − budget = 287 − 241 = 46 g. 241 g is the new budget that stays dissolved (not what leaves), and 287 g is the original amount — only the difference crystallises.

options.text: 0 g
options.label: D

questions.question: 287 g of solid is dissolved at 60°C. On cooling to 50°C the water can hold only 264 g. What mass crystallises out?

evaluation.explanation: excess out = 287 − 264 = 23 g. 264 g is the remaining budget that stays in solution, not the amount that leaves; the answer is the difference between dissolved and budget.

options.text: Crystallisation keeps the water, so impurities stay dissolved in the liquid you pour off
options.label: A

options.text: Boiling destroys the solid you want to keep
options.label: B

options.text: Evaporation can never form any solid at all
options.label: C

options.text: Crystallisation produces more total solid than evaporation
options.label: D

questions.question: You want to recover a PURE solid from a solution containing dissolved impurities. Why is slow crystallisation better than boiling the water completely away?

evaluation.explanation: In crystallisation the lattice accepts only the right particles while impurities remain dissolved in the leftover liquid. Boiling dry removes the water entirely, so the impurities have nowhere to go but get trapped in the solid — it doesn't destroy or fail to form solid, it just makes it impure.

options.text: With all the water gone, the impurities have nowhere to go and get trapped in the dried solid
options.label: A

options.text: Boiling chemically changes the salt into a new substance
options.label: B

options.text: Heat always adds new impurities to a solution
options.label: C

options.text: Salt cannot be recovered by evaporation, only by cooling
options.label: D

questions.question: A student boils a salt solution completely dry to get crystals. The result is less pure than crystallisation would give. What is the reason?

evaluation.explanation: Evaporation drives off all the water, so dissolved impurities are forced into the solid and scattered through it. The salt isn't chemically changed and can be recovered by evaporation — it's just impure because no liquid remains to hold the impurities.

options.text: The regular repeating lattice only accepts particles of the right shape, so impurities are turned away and stay dissolved
options.label: A

options.text: Impurities evaporate away as the solution cools
options.label: B

options.text: Cooling chemically breaks down the impurity particles
options.label: C

options.text: The impurities sink to the bottom and form a separate crystal
options.label: D

questions.question: At the molecular level, why do crystals that form on cooling come out pure?

evaluation.explanation: The lattice is like a 3D jigsaw that only fits pieces of the correct shape, so wrongly-shaped impurity particles can't lock in and remain dissolved in the liquid. They aren't evaporated, destroyed, or formed into their own crystal — they simply stay in solution.

options.text: They slow down and lock into a regular repeating lattice
options.label: A

options.text: They speed up and escape as gas
options.label: B

options.text: They react with the water to form a new compound
options.label: C

options.text: They remain dissolved no matter how much the budget drops
options.label: D

questions.question: As a solution cools slowly, what happens to the surplus particles of the dissolved solid?

evaluation.explanation: Cooling reduces the budget; the surplus particles slow and arrange into an ordered lattice — that's crystallisation. They don't escape as gas or react with water, and once the budget falls below the dissolved amount the excess cannot all stay dissolved.

options.text: The pace: fast drop-out gives small cloudy particles, slow growth gives large well-formed crystals
options.label: A

options.text: Precipitation only works for salts, crystallisation only for sugars
options.label: B

options.text: Precipitation needs heating while crystallisation needs cooling
options.label: C

options.text: Only crystallisation removes solid; precipitation leaves it dissolved
options.label: D

questions.question: Both precipitation and crystallisation shed excess solid from a supersaturated solution. What mainly distinguishes them?

evaluation.explanation: The key difference is speed: rapid precipitation produces many tiny particles (a cloudy powder), while slow crystallisation gives particles time to build large, ordered crystals. Both remove solid and aren't limited to specific substances or fixed temperature changes.

options.text: Cool it slowly so particles have time to find their lattice positions
options.label: A

options.text: Cool it as fast as possible so solid drops out quickly
options.label: B

options.text: Boil it dry rapidly
options.label: C

options.text: Add more impurities to seed the crystals
options.label: D

questions.question: To get large, well-formed, pure crystals from a hot saturated solution, you should:

evaluation.explanation: Slow cooling gives particles time to settle into the lattice, producing bigger, purer crystals. Rapid cooling causes fast drop-out into many small cloudy particles (precipitation), boiling dry traps impurities, and adding impurities reduces purity.

questions.question: Water holds 287 g of solid, saturated at 60 °C. Cooled to 40 °C the solubility ceiling falls to 241 g. How much solid crystallises out?

evaluation.explanation: Out = held − new ceiling = 287 − 241 = 46 g. 241 g is what stays dissolved, not what falls out; 287 g would only fall out if the ceiling dropped to zero.

options.text: 0 g
options.label: D

questions.question: A solution holds 287 g, saturated at 60 °C. At 50 °C the ceiling has dropped to 264 g. How much solid is forced out at 50 °C?

evaluation.explanation: Out = 287 − 264 = 23 g. 264 g is the amount still held; 46 g is the figure at 40 °C, a deeper cool than 50 °C.

options.text: Cold water can hold less dissolved solid, so the excess no longer fits

questions.question: Why does cooling a saturated solution force solid to crystallise out?

evaluation.explanation: The solubility ceiling drops with temperature, so the held amount now exceeds what cold water can keep dissolved. The water and solid amounts don't change — only the room available does.

questions.question: After crystals stop forming during cooling, what is true of the cooler solution?

evaluation.explanation: Crystals stop the instant the held amount equals the new ceiling — that is saturation at the lower temperature. It is not unsaturated, and plenty of solid remains dissolved.

questions.question: A student says 'cooling makes the solid appear, so cooling creates new matter.' What is the correct view?

evaluation.explanation: No new matter is created. Those particles were always present, dissolved; cooling lowers the ceiling so they come back together as visible crystals — conservation of mass holds.

questions.question: In the saturated 60 °C solution before cooling, where are the 287 g of solid?

evaluation.explanation: Saturated and clear means all 287 g are dissolved — held right up to the ceiling with nothing left over. There is no pile or visible solid until cooling drops the ceiling.

questions.question: A mixture contains acetone (bp 56 °C) and water (bp 100 °C). Which separation method is appropriate?

evaluation.explanation: The bps differ by 44 °C, well over the ~25 °C threshold, so simple distillation gives a clean split. Fractional distillation is only needed when bps are closer than ~25 °C; the two are miscible so they neither layer (decant) nor separate by filtration.

questions.question: Two liquids boil at 78 °C and 90 °C. Which technique should you choose?

evaluation.explanation: The gap is only 12 °C, below the ~25 °C rule, so both vaporise together and a fractionating column is needed. Simple distillation would not give a clean split with bps this close, and filtration/decanting only work for solids or immiscible layers.

questions.question: Why can distillation separate a water–ethanol mixture even though it looks like one liquid?

evaluation.explanation: Mixing changes nothing chemical, so each liquid keeps its boiling point and the more volatile one (lower bp) boils off first. There is no chemical change, the liquids are miscible (so density/decanting fails), and filtration only separates solids from liquids.

questions.question: In distillation, what does the condenser do?

evaluation.explanation: The condenser cools the rising vapour so it recondenses and drips into a separate flask as pure liquid. It does not heat (that's the flask), filter, or mix anything.

questions.question: To finish a distillation faster, a student cranks the heat well above the higher boiling point. What goes wrong?

evaluation.explanation: Pushing past the higher bp makes both liquids vaporise, so the collected distillate is no longer pure. Speed is tempting, but the disciplined move is gentle heating just above the lower bp; the lower-bp liquid certainly does not stop boiling.

questions.question: For the purest distillate, the heat should be held:

evaluation.explanation: Holding just above the lower bp vaporises only the more volatile liquid, keeping the distillate pure. Max heat boils both liquids (contamination), heating below the lower bp produces no vapour at all, and the midpoint may still exceed only one bp by chance — the reliable target is just above the lower bp.

evaluation.explanation: Sand is an insoluble solid, so pouring the mixture through filter paper traps it while the liquid passes through. Distillation and fractional columns separate miscible liquids by boiling point, not solids; decanting would leave fine sand in the liquid.

questions.question: Inside a fractionating column, what actually separates the components of the mixture?

evaluation.explanation: Separation comes purely from vapour repeatedly condensing and re-vaporising on each tray as it rises. There is no membrane or filter — the column does not sieve molecules by size, which is the tempting but wrong picture.

questions.question: A student says the column works 'like a sieve, letting small molecules through and trapping big ones.' Why is this wrong?

evaluation.explanation: There is no filtering or membrane. Each obstruction forces condensation and re-vaporisation, so components separate by boiling point — not by being physically strained out by size.

questions.question: Why are the lowest-boiling fractions (like refinery gases) collected at the TOP of the column?

evaluation.explanation: The column is hot at the bottom and cool at the top. A low-boiling-point fraction only condenses at low temperature, so it rises highest before condensing. The top is the COOLEST part, not the hottest — that is the common reversal to avoid.

questions.question: Bitumen comes out at the very bottom of the column. What does this tell you about its boiling point?

evaluation.explanation: The bottom is the hottest region; a fraction collected there must condense at high temperature, meaning a high boiling point. Option A confuses lowest with highest — low-boiling fractions reach the cool top instead.

evaluation.explanation: 210°C falls inside the kerosene band (110–250°C). Diesel only condenses above 250°C, and petrol below 110°C, so neither matches the halfway temperature.

evaluation.explanation: The lower boiling point boils off first: −196°C (nitrogen) is reached before −183°C (oxygen) as the liquid warms. Oxygen, with the higher boiling point, is left behind — option D reverses which boiling point is lower.

questions.question: When should you reach for fractional distillation rather than simple distillation?

evaluation.explanation: Closely-spaced boiling points give a muddy mix in one step, so the many repeated mini-distillations of a column are needed. Widely separated boiling points (option A) can be split by simple distillation in a single heat-and-condense.

evaluation.explanation: With boiling points so close, both components vaporise together over a similar range, so one condensation gives a muddy mixture. A fractionating column repeats the cycle many times to finally separate them — they are still different substances (not identical molecules).

questions.question: Three pigments have affinities to the paper of 25 (Yellow), 55 (Red), 82 (Blue). Reading from the solvent front down to the start line, in what order do the bands appear?

evaluation.explanation: Travel distance follows 1 − affinity/100, so lowest affinity travels furthest. Yellow (25) is nearest the front, Blue (82) lags at the bottom. Option A reverses this by assuming sticky pigments travel fastest.

questions.question: A pigment has an affinity of 70. Using travel fraction = 1 − affinity/100, what fraction of the solvent-front distance does it move?

evaluation.explanation: 1 − 70/100 = 0.30, so it travels 30% of the front distance. Option A (0.70) wrongly uses the affinity directly, which would mean sticky pigments travel furthest.

questions.question: In paper chromatography, which correctly pairs the phases?

evaluation.explanation: The paper is fixed in place (stationary, the 'sticky floor') and the solvent flows through it carrying the mixture (mobile, the 'flowing stream'). The pigments are the analytes being separated, not a phase.

evaluation.explanation: High affinity means the molecule keeps pausing against the surface, so it lags and elutes last. Option A is the classic 'sticky = fast' trap — gripping the surface actually slows a molecule down.

evaluation.explanation: Low affinity = stays in the flowing solvent = fastest = first to the front. Option A inverts the logic: high affinity grips the paper and holds the pigment back, so it comes last. Colour is irrelevant to speed.

questions.question: Using g = (rpm / 4000)² × 1800, what is the effective gravity at 2000 rpm?

options.label: D

questions.question: To separate sand + salt + naphthalene, which step should you do FIRST?

evaluation.explanation: Remove the 'jumper' first while everything is dry: sublime the naphthalene, then dissolve, filter, evaporate. Adding water first (A) would wet the naphthalene and make clean sublimation harder; C and D come later in the sequence.

options.text: Filter the sand → dissolve salt → evaporate water
options.label: A

options.text: Dissolve salt in water → filter off sand → evaporate water to recover salt
options.label: B

options.text: Evaporate water → dissolve salt → filter sand
options.label: C

options.text: Filter sand → evaporate water → dissolve salt
options.label: D

questions.question: For sand + salt + naphthalene, after subliming the naphthalene the remaining steps in correct order are:

evaluation.explanation: You must dissolve the salt first so the sand can be filtered out, then evaporate the salt solution to recover the salt. You cannot filter sand before adding water (A), and evaporating before dissolving (C, D) makes no sense.

options.text: The dirt particles are smaller than the filter's pores
options.label: A

options.text: The filter paper dissolves in water
options.label: B

options.text: The dirt is actually fully dissolved like salt
options.label: C

options.text: Water pushes the dirt back up
options.label: D

questions.question: Muddy water poured through filter paper stays just as cloudy. Why does the dirt slip through?

evaluation.explanation: In a fine suspension each particle is smaller than the filter pores, so it passes straight through. The dirt is not dissolved — it is suspended — and the filter does not dissolve.

options.text: The particles are so light that water molecules keep bumping them and they never sink
options.label: A

options.text: The particles are heavier than water
options.label: B

options.text: The water is too warm to allow settling
options.label: C

options.text: The particles have dissolved completely
options.label: D

questions.question: Why does cloudy river water refuse to settle even after standing for days?

evaluation.explanation: Each fine particle is so light that constant collisions from water molecules keep it suspended, so sedimentation fails. If the particles were heavy they would sink; dissolved particles would not make water cloudy.

options.text: It cancels the like charge on particles so they touch and clump into large flocs
options.label: A

options.text: It dissolves the dirt into the water
options.label: B

options.text: It makes the filter pores smaller
options.label: C

options.text: It pushes the particles up to the surface
options.label: D

questions.question: How does adding alum (fitkari) actually clear the water?

evaluation.explanation: Fine particles carry the same charge and repel each other. The coagulant neutralises that charge, letting them stick together into large flocs that settle and filter out. It does not dissolve the dirt or change the filter.

options.text: Change the particles — make them clump bigger — not the filter
options.label: A

options.text: Change the filter to one with much smaller pores
options.label: B

options.text: Change the water by heating it
options.label: C

options.text: Change the container to a taller one
options.label: D

questions.question: The clever idea behind coagulation is to change which variable?

evaluation.explanation: Since the particles slip through any practical filter, the solution is to enlarge the particles into flocs, not to keep shrinking the filter pores or alter the water or container.

options.text: Coagulate → sediment → decant/filter
options.label: A

options.text: Filter → coagulate → sediment
options.label: B

options.text: Sediment → filter → coagulate
options.label: C

options.text: Decant → coagulate → sediment
options.label: D

questions.question: What is the correct order of steps to clear muddy water?

evaluation.explanation: First add coagulant so particles clump into flocs, then let the heavy flocs sediment, then decant or filter the clear liquid. Filtering or decanting first would do nothing because the particles are still too small.

options.text: Flocs are large and heavy, so gravity makes them sink and the filter can trap them
options.label: A

options.text: Flocs dissolve and disappear into the water
options.label: B

options.text: Flocs become electrically charged and float
options.label: C

options.text: Flocs evaporate with the water
options.label: D

questions.question: After coagulation, why can the flocs now be removed when the original particles could not?

evaluation.explanation: Coagulation engineers the particles up the size scale so gravity and the filter can finally grab them. The flocs do not dissolve, float, or evaporate.

options.text: Coagulation — the acid clumps milk proteins into solids

options.text: Filtration through the milk's surface
options.label: B

options.text: Evaporation of the milk
options.label: C

options.text: Dissolving the proteins into the whey
options.label: D

questions.question: Squeezing lemon into warm milk to make paneer is an example of which process?

evaluation.explanation: The acid acts as a coagulant, clumping dispersed milk proteins into solid paneer and leaving watery whey — the same physics as clearing muddy water. It is not filtration, evaporation, or dissolving.

options.text: A coagulant that cancels the dispersion and lets proteins clump
options.label: A

options.text: A solvent that dissolves the milk proteins
options.label: B

options.text: A filter that strains the milk
options.label: C

options.text: A heat source that boils the milk
options.label: D

questions.question: In making paneer, what role does the lemon or vinegar play?

evaluation.explanation: The acid is the coagulant — it makes the dispersed proteins clump into solid paneer, leaving whey behind. It does not dissolve, filter, or heat the milk.
